title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: Tricking a victim's browser into making unwanted requests to a site where they're authenticated.
fullExplanation: CSRF exploits the trust a website has in the user's browser. If a user is logged into a banking site, an attacker can trick their browser into submitting a money transfer request. The browser automatically includes the session cookie, so the bank thinks it's a legitimate request. The victim doesn't even know it happened.

attackSteps.description: User authenticates to bank.com, receives session cookie
Creates HTML with hidden form that submits to bank.com/transfer
Clicks malicious link or visits compromised site
JavaScript or auto-submit form sends request to bank.com
Session cookie included, bank thinks it's legitimate, transfers money

keyFacts: Exploits automatic cookie inclusion by browsers
Works even if victim doesn't click anything (auto-submit)
GET requests should never change state (idempotent)
CSRF tokens make each request unique and unpredictable
SameSite cookie attribute prevents cross-site requests
Requires victim to be authenticated on target site

defenses.name: CSRF Tokens
defenses.description: Include random token in forms, validate on server. Token not in cookie, so attacker can't access it.

defenses.name: SameSite Cookies
defenses.description: Set SameSite=Strict or Lax to prevent cookies from being sent in cross-site requests

defenses.name: Check Referer Header
defenses.description: Verify request came from your own domain (can be bypassed)

defenses.name: Custom Headers
defenses.description: Require X-Requested-With header (AJAX only, not in forms)

defenses.name: Use POST not GET
defenses.description: GET should be read-only, never change state
